description: Pushups to near-failure with strict form. Chest touches floor, full lockout at top. If hips sag, stop — that's your real number.
action: Get into pushup position. Do as many pushups as you can with perfect form.
context: This is your baseline. The number doesn't matter — what matters is that it's honest. Half-reps and sagging hips are lies you tell yourself. A true count of 8 is worth more than a sloppy count of 20.
constraint_note: Chest must touch the floor on every rep. Arms must fully lock out at the top. If your hips sag or your chest doesn't touch, stop counting. Write your real number.
example: If you get to rep 12 and your hips start sagging on rep 13, your number is 12. Not 13. Not 'about 15.' Twelve.

description: 3 sets of pushups with 60 seconds rest between sets. Write each set's count separately.
action: Do 3 sets of pushups to near-failure. Rest exactly 60 seconds between sets. Use the timer for rest periods.
context: Multiple sets reveal your muscular endurance. The drop-off between sets tells you something — a big drop means your endurance is the bottleneck, not your strength.
constraint_note: Same form rules: chest to floor, full lockout. Write all 3 numbers separately (e.g., '15 / 11 / 8'). The drop-off pattern is useful data.
example: Set 1: 15. Set 2: 11. Set 3: 8. That's a 47% drop — endurance is the limiter. Over weeks, the gap between sets should shrink.

description: 10 slow tempo pushups — 3 seconds down, 1 second pause at bottom, 3 seconds up. Write how many you completed with full tempo.
action: Do pushups at a controlled tempo. Count '1-2-3' on the way down, pause for 1 second with chest near the floor, then '1-2-3' on the way up.
context: Tempo removes momentum. You can't bounce out of the bottom or use gravity to cheat. This is where real strength lives — in the slow, controlled movement that most people skip.
constraint_note: If you speed up or lose the tempo, stop counting. The goal is 10, but an honest 6 at full tempo beats a rushed 10. Write how many you completed at true tempo.
example: You might do 15 regular pushups but only manage 7 at this tempo. That gap is the difference between momentum and actual strength.

description: Pike pushups: feet elevated on a chair, hands on floor, body in an inverted V. Do as many as you can. Write the count.
action: Place your feet on a chair or bed. Walk your hands back until your body forms an inverted V shape. Bend your elbows and lower the top of your head toward the floor, then press back up.
context: Pike pushups shift the load to your shoulders. They're the progression toward handstand pushups and build the overhead pressing strength that regular pushups miss entirely.
constraint_note: Head should come close to the floor between your hands. Keep your hips high — don't let it turn into a regular pushup. If your form breaks, stop. Write your count.
example: If you can do 20 regular pushups but only 5 pike pushups, that's normal. Your shoulders are weaker than your chest. That's exactly what this fixes.

description: Diamond pushups: hands together under your chest, thumbs and index fingers forming a diamond. Do as many as you can with full range of motion.
action: Place your hands close together under your chest so your thumbs and index fingers form a diamond shape. Do pushups from this position.
context: Diamond pushups hammer your triceps and inner chest. They're significantly harder than regular pushups — expect your count to drop by 40-50%. That's the point.
constraint_note: Chest must touch your hands at the bottom. Full lockout at top. Elbows stay relatively close to your body. Write your count.
example: Regular pushup count: 20. Diamond pushup count: 9. That's typical. The triceps are the weak link for most people.

description: Pause pushups: lower yourself to the bottom, hold for 2 full seconds, then press up. Write how many you completed before losing the pause.
action: Do pushups, but at the bottom of each rep, hold your chest just off the floor for a full 2-second count before pressing back up.
context: The pause eliminates the stretch reflex — the bounce that makes the bottom of a pushup easier. Without it, your muscles do all the work. This builds starting strength and control.
constraint_note: Count 'one-thousand-one, one-thousand-two' at the bottom. Chest hovers just above the floor. If you can't hold the pause, that rep doesn't count. Write your count.
example: The first 5 feel manageable. By rep 8, the pause becomes brutal. That's where the growth happens.

description: Incline pushups on a counter or sturdy table. 15 reps with perfect form. Write whether you completed all 15 and what felt different from floor pushups.
action: Place your hands on a counter, table, or sturdy elevated surface. Do 15 pushups with the same form standards as floor pushups.
context: Incline pushups aren't just 'easier pushups.' They change the angle of force on your chest and shoulders. They're also the right starting point if floor pushups are too hard — build up here first, then move to the floor.
constraint_note: Chest must touch the surface. Full lockout. Body stays straight — no sagging hips, no piking up. Write whether you completed all 15 and one thing you noticed about the difference from floor pushups.
example: You might notice your lower chest works harder, or that your wrists feel better. Both are useful observations.

description: Pushup hold at the bottom position. Lower yourself until your chest is 2 inches from the floor and hold. Time it. Write the number.
action: Get into pushup position. Lower yourself slowly until your chest is about 2 inches from the floor. Hold that position as long as you can.
context: Isometric holds build strength at the weakest point of the movement — the bottom. Most people are weakest here because they never spend time here. This fixes that.
constraint_note: Body must stay straight. Chest stays 2 inches from the floor — not resting on it. If your body touches the floor or your hips sag, time stops. Write your hold time in seconds.
example: 30 seconds feels easy. 45 seconds starts shaking. 60 seconds is genuinely hard. Your number tells you where your bottom-position strength is.

description: 20 bodyweight squats with the deepest range of motion you can manage while keeping heels flat. Write how low you got.
action: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Squat down as low as you can, then stand back up. Do 20 reps.
context: Depth is the honest metric here. Anyone can do 20 quarter-squats. Going below parallel — hips below knees — requires ankle mobility, hip flexibility, and real leg strength. Your depth tells you where your limits are.
constraint_note: Heels must stay flat on the floor the entire time. If your heels lift, that's your depth limit. Write how low you got: quarter, parallel (thighs horizontal), below parallel, or full (hamstrings touch calves).
example: If you can only reach parallel before your heels lift, that's an ankle mobility issue. If you can go deep but your back rounds, that's a hip mobility issue. Both are useful to know.

description: Wall sit: back flat against wall, thighs parallel to floor. Time it. If your thighs creep above parallel, that's your real time.
action: Stand with your back against a wall. Slide down until your thighs are parallel to the floor, knees at 90 degrees. Hold.
context: Wall sits are pure isometric leg endurance. There's nowhere to hide — your quads are either holding or they're not. The shaking at 45 seconds is your nervous system recruiting every available muscle fiber.
constraint_note: Back stays flat against the wall. Thighs must be parallel to the floor — not higher. If your thighs creep above parallel, time stops. Write your hold time in seconds.
example: 30 seconds: warming up. 60 seconds: working. 90 seconds: genuinely hard. 120 seconds: strong legs.

description: Bulgarian split squats: back foot on a chair, 10 reps each leg. Write whether you completed all 10 per leg and which side was weaker.
action: Stand in front of a chair. Place one foot behind you on the chair. Lower your back knee toward the floor, then press up. Do 10 reps, then switch legs.
context: Single-leg work exposes imbalances. Most people have one leg significantly stronger than the other. Bulgarian split squats fix that — and they build the kind of functional leg strength that bilateral squats miss.
constraint_note: Back knee should come close to the floor on each rep. Front knee stays over your toes, not caving inward. Write whether you completed all 10 per leg and which side felt weaker.
example: Right leg: 10 reps, felt stable. Left leg: 8 reps, knee wobbled on the last 3. The wobble is the signal — that's the side that needs more work.

description: Single-leg squat to chair: stand on one leg, sit down slowly (3 seconds), stand back up. How many per leg?
action: Stand on one leg in front of a chair. Slowly lower yourself to sit on the chair (3 seconds down), then stand back up on the same leg. Do as many as you can per leg.
context: This is the pistol squat progression. The chair catches you at the bottom so you can focus on the controlled descent. The 3-second tempo prevents you from just falling into the chair.
constraint_note: 3 seconds down — count it. The non-working leg stays off the floor. If you need to touch the other foot down for balance, that rep doesn't count. Write your count for each leg.
example: Right leg: 6. Left leg: 4. The difference tells you about your balance and single-leg strength asymmetry.

description: 15 jump squats. Squat to parallel, explode up, land softly. Write how many you completed with genuinely soft landings.
action: Squat down to parallel, then explode upward as high as you can. Land softly — toes first, then heels, absorbing the impact with your legs.
context: Jump squats add power to your leg training. But the landing is where most people get hurt. A soft, controlled landing means your muscles are absorbing force. A loud, jarring landing means your joints are.
constraint_note: The landing must be quiet. If someone in the next room can hear you land, you're not absorbing the impact properly. Squat to at least parallel before each jump. Write how many of the 15 had genuinely soft landings.
example: 15 jumps, 11 soft landings. The last 4 got sloppy because your legs were tired. That's honest data.

description: Lunge walk: 10 steps each leg. Back knee must touch or nearly touch the floor. Write whether you kept balance on all 20 steps.
action: Step forward into a lunge. Lower your back knee until it touches or nearly touches the floor. Step through into the next lunge. 10 steps per leg, 20 total.
context: Walking lunges combine strength, balance, and coordination. They expose weaknesses that static exercises hide — if your knee wobbles or you lose balance mid-step, that's a stability gap.
constraint_note: Back knee must come within an inch of the floor on every step. Front knee stays over your ankle, not shooting past your toes. Write whether you completed all 20 steps without losing balance.
example: All 20 steps, but lost balance on step 14 (left leg forward). That tells you your left hip stabilizers are the weak link.

description: Squat hold at the bottom: squat as low as you can with heels flat and hold. Time it. Write your time and what felt tight.
action: Squat down as low as you can while keeping your heels flat on the floor. Hold that position.
context: The deep squat is a resting position in most of the world. If you can't hold it for 60 seconds, you've lost a basic human movement pattern. This hold rebuilds ankle and hip mobility simultaneously.
constraint_note: Heels flat. Back as upright as you can manage. If you fall backward or your heels lift, that's your time. Write your hold time and what felt tightest — ankles, hips, or lower back.
example: 42 seconds, ankles felt tightest. That means ankle dorsiflexion is the bottleneck. Calf stretches and ankle mobility work will help.

description: Forearm plank: body straight, hold as long as you can. If your hips sag or pike up, that's your real time. Write the number.
action: Get into a forearm plank — elbows under shoulders, body in a straight line from head to heels. Hold.
context: The plank is the most honest core exercise. There's no momentum, no cheating, no way to fake it. Your time is your time. And the form constraint is what makes it real — a 90-second plank with sagging hips is a lower back injury waiting to happen.
constraint_note: Body must be a straight line. If your hips sag toward the floor or pike up toward the ceiling, time stops. Squeeze your glutes and brace your abs like someone's about to poke your stomach. Write your hold time in seconds.
example: 45 seconds with perfect form is better than 2 minutes with a sagging back. The form IS the exercise.

description: Hollow body hold: lie on your back, arms overhead, legs straight, lift shoulders and feet 6 inches off the floor. Time it.
action: Lie on your back. Extend your arms overhead and your legs straight out. Press your lower back into the floor, then lift your shoulders and feet about 6 inches off the ground. Hold.
context: The hollow body is a gymnast's foundation. It trains the anterior core in a way planks can't — full tension from fingertips to toes. If your lower back lifts off the floor, your core has given up and your hip flexors have taken over.
constraint_note: Lower back must stay pressed into the floor the entire time. If it arches up, time stops. Arms stay by your ears, legs stay straight. Write your hold time in seconds.
example: 20 seconds is respectable for a beginner. 45 seconds means your core is genuinely strong. If you can't hold 10 seconds, bend your knees to make it easier.

description: Dead bugs: lie on your back, arms up, knees at 90°. Extend opposite arm and leg slowly. 10 each side. Write whether your lower back stayed flat.
action: Lie on your back with arms pointing at the ceiling and knees bent at 90 degrees. Slowly extend your right arm overhead and left leg straight out, hovering above the floor. Return and switch sides. 10 reps each side.
context: Dead bugs train anti-extension — your core's ability to prevent your lower back from arching under load. This is the core strength that protects your spine in real life, not the six-pack muscle.
constraint_note: Your lower back must stay flat against the floor the entire time. If it arches, you've gone too far. Move slowly — 2 seconds out, 2 seconds back. Write whether your lower back stayed flat on all 20 reps.
example: If your back arches when your leg extends, don't extend as far. The range of motion where your back stays flat IS your working range.

description: Side plank: hold each side. Time both. Write both numbers and which side was weaker.
action: Lie on your side, prop yourself up on your forearm, and lift your hips so your body forms a straight line. Hold. Then switch sides.
constraint_note: Hips must stay stacked — don't let them rotate forward or sag toward the floor. Body is a straight line from head to feet. Write both times and which side was weaker.
context: Side planks train the obliques and lateral stabilizers that front planks miss. Almost everyone has a weaker side. The asymmetry tells you something about your daily movement patterns.
example: Right side: 50 seconds. Left side: 35 seconds. The 15-second gap means your left obliques need more work. This is common if you always carry bags on one side.

description: Leg raises: lie on your back, legs straight, raise to 90° and lower slowly. Do as many as you can without your lower back lifting. Write the count.
action: Lie on your back with legs straight. Raise both legs to 90 degrees, then lower them slowly back toward the floor. Don't let them touch the floor between reps.
context: Leg raises look like a leg exercise but they're a core exercise. The challenge is keeping your lower back on the floor as your legs descend. When your back arches, your hip flexors have taken over and your core has quit.
constraint_note: Lower back stays on the floor. Lower your legs as far as you can while keeping your back flat — that's your working range. If your back arches, stop the set. Write your count.
example: You might lower your legs to 6 inches from the floor on rep 1, but only to 45 degrees by rep 10. That's your core fatiguing. The honest count is when your back first lifts.

description: Mountain climbers: 30 seconds as fast as you can with good form. Write how many reps you counted and whether your hips stayed level.
action: Get into a pushup position. Drive one knee toward your chest, then quickly switch legs. Go as fast as you can for 30 seconds.
context: Mountain climbers combine core stability with cardio. The core challenge is keeping your hips level and your back flat while your legs move fast. When form breaks, it becomes a hip flexor exercise instead of a core exercise.
constraint_note: Hips stay level — no bouncing up and down. Back stays flat. Each knee must come to at least your elbow. Count one rep per leg. Write your total count and whether your hips stayed level the whole time.
example: 40 reps in 30 seconds with level hips is solid. 60 reps with bouncing hips is just cardio with bad form.

description: Bird dog: on all fours, extend opposite arm and leg. Hold 5 seconds each side, 8 reps total. Write whether you wobbled.
action: Get on all fours — hands under shoulders, knees under hips. Extend your right arm forward and left leg back simultaneously. Hold for 5 seconds. Return and switch sides. 4 reps each side.
context: Bird dogs train anti-rotation — your core's ability to resist twisting. They look easy but done slowly with a 5-second hold, they expose every stability weakness. Physical therapists use these to rehab backs for a reason.
constraint_note: Your hips must stay square to the floor — no rotation. Extended arm and leg should be parallel to the floor. Hold each rep for a full 5-second count. Write whether you wobbled or rotated on any rep.
example: If your hip drops when you extend your leg, your glute medius is weak. If you rotate, your obliques need work. The wobble IS the diagnosis.

description: Reverse plank: sit on the floor, hands behind you, lift your hips until your body is a straight line. Time it. Write the number.
action: Sit on the floor with legs extended. Place your hands behind you, fingers pointing toward your feet. Press up until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to heels. Hold.
context: Reverse planks train the posterior chain — glutes, lower back, and rear shoulders — that front planks completely miss. Most people are shockingly weak here because they never train this position.
constraint_note: Hips must stay up — body in a straight line. If your hips sag, time stops. Squeeze your glutes hard. Write your hold time in seconds.
example: If you can hold a front plank for 90 seconds but a reverse plank for only 20, your posterior core is the weak link. That imbalance matters.

description: Deep squat hold: squat as low as you can, heels flat, hold for 60 seconds. Write what felt tight and where you felt the stretch most.
action: Squat down as deep as you can with your heels flat on the floor. Hold the bottom position for 60 seconds. You can hold onto a doorframe or table for balance if needed.
context: The deep squat is a resting position for most of the world's population. If you can't hold it comfortably, you've lost a fundamental human movement. This hold rebuilds ankle, hip, and thoracic mobility simultaneously.
constraint_note: Heels stay flat. Try to keep your torso as upright as possible. Holding something for balance is fine — the goal is time in the position, not balance. Write what felt tightest: ankles, hips, groin, or lower back.
example: If your ankles are the bottleneck, you'll feel a strong stretch in your calves. If it's your hips, you'll feel it in your groin. The tightest area is where you need the most work.

description: Shoulder dislocates with a towel: hold wide, arms straight, bring from front to behind your back. 10 reps. Write how wide your grip needed to be.
action: Hold a towel or broomstick with both hands, arms straight in front of you. Keeping your arms straight, bring it up and over your head, all the way behind your back, then return. Do 10 reps.
context: Shoulder dislocates are the single best shoulder mobility exercise. They open up the chest, improve overhead range of motion, and undo the damage from sitting hunched over a desk. The grip width is your mobility score.
constraint_note: Arms must stay straight the entire time — no bending elbows. Start with a wide grip and narrow it as you warm up. Write the narrowest grip width where you could complete the full rotation (e.g., 'shoulder width + 6 inches').
example: Week 1: need a very wide grip. Week 4: hands are shoulder-width apart. That narrowing IS the progress.

description: Couch stretch: kneel with back foot against a wall, front foot forward. 60 seconds each side. Write which side was tighter.
action: Kneel in front of a wall. Place one foot against the wall behind you (shin flat against the wall). Step the other foot forward into a lunge position. Squeeze the glute of the back leg and hold. 60 seconds each side.
context: The couch stretch targets your hip flexors and quads — the muscles that get shortened from sitting all day. Tight hip flexors cause lower back pain, poor posture, and weak glutes. This is the antidote.
constraint_note: Squeeze the glute of the back leg — this deepens the stretch and protects your lower back. Stay upright, don't lean forward. Write which side was tighter and rate the intensity 1-10.
example: Right side: 7/10 intensity, felt deep in the front of the hip. Left side: 5/10, more manageable. The tighter side is the one that needs more attention.

description: Seated forward fold: legs straight, reach for your toes, hold for 60 seconds. Write where your hands reached — shins, ankles, toes, or past toes.
action: Sit on the floor with legs straight in front of you. Reach forward toward your toes. Hold the deepest comfortable position for 60 seconds.
context: Hamstring flexibility affects everything — your squat depth, your lower back health, your running form. This simple test tells you exactly where you are. The number is honest and it changes over weeks.
constraint_note: Keep your legs straight — no bending knees. Reach as far as you can without bouncing. Write where your hands reached: mid-shin, ankles, toes, or past toes.
example: Mid-shin: tight hamstrings, common for desk workers. Toes: average flexibility. Past toes: good flexibility. Track this monthly — it moves slowly but it moves.

description: Single-leg balance: stand on one foot, eyes closed. Time each side. Write both numbers.
action: Stand on one foot. Close your eyes. Hold as long as you can without touching the other foot down. Then switch sides.
context: Balance with eyes closed removes visual compensation and tests your proprioception — your body's sense of where it is in space. This declines with age faster than strength does, and it's the skill that prevents falls.
constraint_note: Eyes must be closed. If your other foot touches the floor, time stops. Arms can be wherever feels natural. Write both times.
example: Right: 22 seconds. Left: 14 seconds. The asymmetry matters. Under 15 seconds with eyes closed suggests proprioception needs work.

description: Hip circles: stand on one leg, draw large slow circles with the other knee. 10 each direction, each leg. Write what felt restricted.
action: Stand on one leg. Lift the other knee to hip height. Slowly draw large circles with your knee — 10 clockwise, then 10 counterclockwise. Switch legs.
context: Hip circles reveal restrictions in your hip joint's range of motion. Grinding, clicking, or tightness in certain positions tells you exactly where your hip mobility is limited. Most people have no idea how restricted their hips are until they try this.
constraint_note: Circles should be as large as your range of motion allows. Move slowly — 2-3 seconds per circle. Note any positions where you feel grinding, clicking, or restriction. Write what you noticed for each leg.
example: Right hip: smooth circles, slight tightness at the back. Left hip: clicking sound when the knee passes the midline. The clicking suggests a mobility restriction worth addressing.

description: Thoracic rotation: lie on your side, knees stacked, rotate your top arm open like a book. 10 reps each side. Write which side was more restricted.
action: Lie on your side with knees bent and stacked. Extend both arms in front of you, palms together. Keeping your knees stacked, slowly rotate your top arm up and over, opening your chest toward the ceiling. Follow your hand with your eyes. Return. 10 reps each side.
context: Thoracic rotation is the mobility most desk workers have lost. When your upper back can't rotate, your lower back compensates — and that's where pain comes from. This exercise directly addresses the root cause.
constraint_note: Knees must stay stacked — don't let them separate. The rotation comes from your upper back, not your lower back. Move slowly and breathe out as you open. Write which side was more restricted and whether you felt any tightness.
example: If your hand can't reach the floor on one side, that's a significant thoracic restriction. Most people are tighter on the side they sleep on.
